jokes.text: Well, I, for one, would like to meet this Jackie Denardo and see if her breasts are as staggering in person.
jokes.setup: Serious hurricane news coverage with female weather reporter
jokes.punchline: Dennis completely ignoring the disaster to focus on the reporter's breasts
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Dennis's vanity and objectification on full display during a crisis. Strong setup/payoff with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Well, I, for one, would like to meet this Jackie Denardo and see if her breasts are as staggering in person.

jokes.text: Guys, maybe we should get a 3-D TV. The breasts are only going to keep getting bigger, and we need the latest technology to keep up.
jokes.setup: Following Dennis's comment about the reporter's breasts
jokes.punchline: Mac's completely illogical reasoning that breasts are getting bigger and they need technology to 'keep up'
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist escalation. Mac's twisted logic about needing 3-D TV to 'keep up' with breast evolution is perfectly stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Guys, maybe we should get a 3-D TV. The breasts are only going to keep getting bigger, and we need the latest technology to keep up.

jokes.text: Oh, she's talking about it and, you know, we're sort of listening and we're enjoying her breasts. Just let us have this. The breasts make you care about the weather in a really good way
jokes.setup: Dee criticizing their focus on the reporter's breasts during a hurricane
jokes.punchline: Charlie's pathetic justification that breasts make weather more interesting
jokes.explanation: Charlie's weird earnestness about breast-enhanced weather consumption is perfectly in character and appropriately pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Oh, she's talking about it and, you know, we're sort of listening and we're enjoying her breasts. Just let us have this. The breasts make you care about the weather in a really good way

jokes.text: Amigos, tenemos... Ho! Whoo! Cha, cha, cha. Oh, man, it's internacional. It's on every channel. These are the breasts we should focus on.
jokes.setup: Switching to Spanish weather channel to avoid breast fixation
jokes.punchline: Spanish channel also has busty weather reporter, complete with exaggerated Spanish reactions
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation of the breast obsession theme. The fake Spanish and 'internacional' pronunciation add visual comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Amigos, tenemos... Ho! Whoo! Cha, cha, cha. Oh, man, it's internacional. It's on every channel. These are the breasts we should focus on.

jokes.text: What are you wearing? Oh, I got all riled up by the weather report and ain't a drop of rain out there, so I put this old trash bag on to keep myself protected.
jokes.setup: Frank appears in scene unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: Frank is wearing a trash bag as storm protection despite no actual rain
jokes.explanation: Great visual gag that perfectly captures Frank's paranoid overreaction. The contrast between his preparation and reality is well-crafted.

dialogue.line: What are you wearing?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Oh, I got all riled up by the weather report and ain't a drop of rain out there, so I put this old trash bag on to keep myself protected.

jokes.text: That was the Mayans, dude. / Mayans, Mexicans-- what's the difference? / One difference is that the Mayans are extinct. / Yeah, the Mexicans killed the Mayans. / No, the Spaniards banged the Mayans, turned 'em into Mexicans.
jokes.setup: Mac confusing Mayans with Mexicans about 2012 predictions
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous explanations culminating in Charlie's bizarre historical summary
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating exchange with perfect character voices. Charlie's 'banged them into Mexicans' is memorably absurd and historically ignorant.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: That was the Mayans, dude.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Mayans, Mexicans-- what's the difference?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: One difference is that the Mayans are extinct.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah, the Mexicans killed the Mayans.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: No, the Spaniards banged the Mayans, turned 'em into Mexicans.

jokes.text: We got to stop pussydicking around here, okay? It's time to get in the bunker.
jokes.setup: Dennis trying to take charge of hurricane preparation
jokes.punchline: Dennis using the made-up word 'pussydicking' while trying to sound authoritative
jokes.explanation: Dennis's attempt to sound commanding while using a ridiculous made-up word perfectly captures his delusional self-image.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: We got to stop pussydicking around here, okay? It's time to get in the bunker.

jokes.text: Yeah, we, uh, we converted this old storage closet into a place where we could ride out Y2K.
jokes.setup: Gang discussing their emergency bunker
jokes.punchline: Revealing they built it for Y2K, showing their history of overreacting to media hype
jokes.explanation: Good setup for their pattern of panic. Y2K reference dates them perfectly and explains their preparedness paranoia.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah, we, uh, we converted this old storage closet into a place where we could ride out Y2K.

jokes.text: Yeah, robopocalypse-- machines communicating with each other and conspiring to destroy the human race.
jokes.setup: Discussion of why they were scared of Y2K
jokes.punchline: Dee's dramatic description of robot uprising using made-up word 'robopocalypse'
jokes.explanation: Dee's conspiracy theory language with 'robopocalypse' is perfectly dramatic and paranoid. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Yeah, robopocalypse-- machines communicating with each other and conspiring to destroy the human race.

jokes.text: And why is there a condom wrapper on the floor? / Uh, you've probably been banging in the bunker, right? / Guilty. / I've been watching him bang in here.
jokes.setup: Discovering evidence of sexual activity in their emergency bunker
jokes.punchline: Frank casually admitting to watching Charlie have sex in the bunker
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from finding a condom to Frank's disturbing voyeurism admission. Great cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: And why is there a condom wrapper on the floor?

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Uh, you've probably been banging in the bunker, right?

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Guilty.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I've been watching him bang in here.

jokes.text: I don't rape girls, come on. / Yeah, and I don't eat that many pickled eggs, but...
jokes.setup: Mac accusing Charlie of raping girls in the bunker
jokes.punchline: Charlie and Frank treating rape accusation and pickled egg consumption as equivalent denials
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - the casual equivalence between serious accusations and trivial eating habits is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I don't rape girls, come on.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I don't eat that many pickled eggs, but...

jokes.text: And they have left, they have left now. I'm only still here 'cause I'm not as quick as them.
jokes.setup: Gang suddenly abandons hurricane preparation to go shopping
jokes.punchline: Charlie matter-of-factly explaining he's only still there because he's slow
jokes.explanation: Charlie's deadpan self-awareness about his slowness is perfectly delivered. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: And they have left, they have left now. I'm only still here 'cause I'm not as quick as them.

jokes.text: Is that a pickled egg? / No, it's an egg I brought from home.
jokes.setup: Frank questioning Charlie about eating emergency supplies
jokes.punchline: Charlie's distinction between pickled eggs and regular eggs he brought from home makes no sense in context
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency - Charlie's weird food obsessions and illogical distinctions. Nice callback to pickled eggs.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Is that a pickled egg?

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: No, it's an egg I brought from home.

jokes.text: Hey, you know what I was thinking? In addition to supplies, might be nice to grab some girls, you know, get them back to the bar. This Jackie Denardo chick-- she's, uh, sparked another storm inside me if you know what I mean.
jokes.setup: Dennis supposedly focused on hurricane supplies
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals his real priority is picking up women using cheesy sexual metaphor
jokes.explanation: Dennis's priorities shifting to women is perfectly in character. The storm metaphor is cheesy in a way that fits his personality.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what I was thinking? In addition to supplies, might be nice to grab some girls, you know, get them back to the bar. This Jackie Denardo chick-- she's, uh, sparked another storm inside me if you know what I mean.

jokes.text: Yeah, yeah, and we could use 'em to repopulate the planet or something.
jokes.setup: Dennis suggesting they bring women to the bar
jokes.punchline: Mac immediately jumping to post-apocalyptic breeding scenarios
jokes.explanation: Mac's leap to species survival justification for picking up women is perfectly absurd escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, and we could use 'em to repopulate the planet or something.

jokes.text: Okay, I'd at least like to find one, like, Mexican or Mayan or something for predictions. / No, but get some batteries, though. You be the battery guy and I'll be the, the chicks and tits guy.
jokes.setup: Dennis rejecting Mac's breeding ideas
jokes.punchline: Mac wanting a Mexican/Mayan fortune teller and Dennis's division of labor as 'chicks and tits guy'
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier confusion. Dennis's job titles are perfectly crude and direct.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Okay, I'd at least like to find one, like, Mexican or Mayan or something for predictions.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: No, but get some batteries, though. You be the battery guy and I'll be the, the chicks and tits guy.

jokes.text: When it's white people, it's survival, and when it's black people, it's looting. / No, Frank, it's because the white people are stealing bread, and the black people are stealing speakers.
jokes.setup: Frank pointing out media bias in disaster coverage
jokes.punchline: Dee completely missing the point and reinforcing the stereotype Frank was criticizing
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly crafted commentary on media bias where Dee's response perfectly demonstrates the racism Frank was exposing. Sharp social satire.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: When it's white people, it's survival, and when it's black people, it's looting.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: No, Frank, it's because the white people are stealing bread, and the black people are stealing speakers.

jokes.text: How do you know the blacks don't have bread in those speakers?
jokes.setup: Dee's explanation about different types of looting
jokes.punchline: Frank's completely absurd suggestion that speakers might contain bread
jokes.explanation: Frank's logic is so absurd it becomes brilliant. The idea of hiding bread in speakers is memorably ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: How do you know the blacks don't have bread in those speakers?

jokes.text: I happened to be out in L.A. during the riots. I was on business. / And to steal ski equipment, I guess. / Yeah, well, I went in for a loaf of bread, I took some skis. Everybody's happy.
jokes.setup: Frank showing video of himself during LA riots
jokes.punchline: Frank casually admitting to looting skis while claiming he went for bread
jokes.explanation: Frank's casual admission to riot looting while maintaining innocence is perfectly sociopathic. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I happened to be out in L.A. during the riots. I was on business.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: And to steal ski equipment, I guess.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I went in for a loaf of bread, I took some skis. Everybody's happy.

jokes.text: What's a hurricane party? / A hurricane party's exactly what it sounds like. It's a party during a hurricane at our bar, Paddy's Pub.
jokes.setup: Dennis trying to explain their hurricane party concept
jokes.punchline: Dennis's completely unhelpful circular definition that explains nothing
jokes.explanation: Dennis's non-explanation is perfectly unhelpful in a way that shows his social incompetence despite thinking he's smooth.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: What's a hurricane party?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: A hurricane party's exactly what it sounds like. It's a party during a hurricane at our bar, Paddy's Pub.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, he was probably down here with Mac, watching you rape girls and eating pickled eggs. I don't rape girls, come on. Yeah, and I don't eat that many pickled eggs, but...
jokes.setup: Frank's accusation about Charlie's bunker activities
jokes.punchline: Charlie denies rape but only moderates the pickled egg consumption claim

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I don't rape girls, come on. Yeah, and I don't eat that many pickled eggs, but...

jokes.text: Yeah, we got a sweet bunker, too. / Yeah, well... It's, like, so secure, people can't get in or out if we don't want 'em to.
jokes.setup: Trying to impress women with their bar amenities
jokes.punchline: Charlie describing their bunker in terms that sound like a kidnapping setup
jokes.explanation: Charlie's description accidentally sounds sinister - perfect example of their social incompetence making them seem dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Yeah, we got a sweet bunker, too.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... It's, like, so secure, people can't get in or out if we don't want 'em to.

jokes.text: How did you not know... that the reason I invited you back to my bar... was to bang you? Get out of here! Get the hell out of here! Get... Get out of here! Shoo away from me!
jokes.setup: Women mentioning they have boyfriends
jokes.punchline: Mac completely losing it and literally shooing them away like animals when his pickup plan fails
jokes.explanation: Mac's complete social meltdown and treating women like animals perfectly captures his entitlement and incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: How did you not know... that the reason I invited you back to my bar... was to bang you? Get out of here! Get the hell out of here! Get... Get out of here! Shoo away from me!

jokes.text: Dude, they have amazing stuff here. I'm totally prepared. Check this out. How sweet is that? / You think girls are gonna want to come back to a bar with a man dressed the way you're dressed and carrying a hatchet?
jokes.setup: Charlie showing off his hurricane preparation gear
jokes.punchline: Dennis pointing out that Charlie looks like a psycho killer to potential dates
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Charlie's pride in his gear and Dennis's accurate assessment of how threatening he looks.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Dude, they have amazing stuff here. I'm totally prepared. Check this out. How sweet is that?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: You think girls are gonna want to come back to a bar with a man dressed the way you're dressed and carrying a hatchet?

jokes.text: Yeah. They're gonna be, like, 'Look at this sweet dude, his awesome gear and his awesome hatchet. He's prepared, he's ready for anything. Storm coming? Hatchet coming.'
jokes.setup: Dennis questioning Charlie's hatchet as pickup gear
jokes.punchline: Charlie's completely delusional fantasy of women finding his hatchet attractive, ending with 'Storm coming? Hatchet coming.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Charlie logic and catchphrase potential. His complete misreading of how women would react to a hatchet is brilliantly delusional.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Yeah. They're gonna be, like, 'Look at this sweet dude, his awesome gear and his awesome hatchet. He's prepared, he's ready for anything. Storm coming? Hatchet coming.'

jokes.text: It's Cricket! / Cricket. / Oh, what are you doing down here, Cricket? / I come down the bunker sometimes when it rains.
jokes.setup: Gang finds someone in their bunker who they mistake for a looter
jokes.punchline: It's Cricket, the homeless former priest, casually using their bunker as shelter
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency with Cricket's degraded state. His casual use of their 'emergency' bunker as regular shelter is appropriately pathetic.

dialogue.line: It's Cricket!

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Cricket.

dialogue.line: Oh, what are you doing down here, Cricket?

dialogue.speaker: Cricket

jokes.text: No, I ain't going to no hospital. They euthanize the homeless. They're goddamned death camps!

jokes.punchline: Cricket's paranoid belief that hospitals kill homeless people
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about healthcare and homelessness. Cricket's paranoia is both funny and tragically plausible given his situation.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: No, I ain't going to no hospital. They euthanize the homeless. They're goddamned death camps!

jokes.text: We could dress him up and take him upstairs, and then the looters would think he's the owner of the bar, and they'd have their way with him, and if he died, you know, it's on them, and-and not on us.
jokes.setup: Discussing what to do about Cricket's injury
jokes.punchline: Dee's elaborate plan to use Cricket as a decoy/sacrifice for looters
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark plan that shows Dee's complete lack of empathy. The casual way she plans Cricket's potential death is perfectly sociopathic.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: We could dress him up and take him upstairs, and then the looters would think he's the owner of the bar, and they'd have their way with him, and if he died, you know, it's on them, and-and not on us.

jokes.text: You're marching me to the ovens.
jokes.setup: Gang finally agreeing to take Cricket to the hospital
jokes.punchline: Cricket comparing hospital trip to Holocaust death camps
jokes.explanation: Dark reference that fits Cricket's paranoid worldview. The Holocaust reference is harsh but consistent with his mental state.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: You're marching me to the ovens.

jokes.text: Being of sound mind and body, do solemnly pledge that I do not have a boyfriend. Nor am I currently engaged in a sexual relationship with another individual, females excluded.
jokes.setup: Dennis showing Charlie his contract for women
jokes.punchline: The formal legal language applied to crude sexual requirements, plus the lesbian exception
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dennis - combining his legal pretensions with sexual entitlement. The lesbian exception shows his predictable male fantasy thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Being of sound mind and body, do solemnly pledge that I do not have a boyfriend. Nor am I currently engaged in a sexual relationship with another individual, females excluded.

jokes.text: I hereby agree to be easygoing, to engage in playful conversation, to always act as though I desire your penis even when I don't.
jokes.setup: Continuing to read his contract for women
jokes.punchline: The requirement that women fake desire for his penis even when they don't want it
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Dennis's narcissism and complete lack of understanding about consent or attraction. Deeply cringeworthy in the best way.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: I hereby agree to be easygoing, to engage in playful conversation, to always act as though I desire your penis even when I don't.

jokes.text: I love it. Where do I sign? / Okay, again, um, you don't sign anywhere. / If we're doing a contract, I want to sign it, so... This isn't a contract for you, Charlie.

jokes.punchline: Charlie wanting to sign the contract meant for women, completely missing the point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Charlie logic - his childlike excitement about signing any contract regardless of what it says. Great character consistency.

dialogue.line: Okay, again, um, you don't sign anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: If we're doing a contract, I want to sign it, so...

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: This isn't a contract for you, Charlie.

I'd start sleeping with your daughters, you'd sleep with my daughters, I'd sleep with your daughters' daughters, you'd sleep with my daughters' daughters. We die, our sons would sleep with our daughters' daughters' daughters.

jokes.punchline: Charlie's detailed incestuous breeding plan spanning multiple generations
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly disturbing escalation. Charlie's matter-of-fact planning of generational incest is perfectly absurd and memorable.

I'd start sleeping with your daughters, you'd sleep with my daughters, I'd sleep with your daughters' daughters, you'd sleep with my daughters' daughters. jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Charlie's food obsessions and lack of self-control on full display. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Have you gotten into the cheese?

dialogue.speaker: Charlie

jokes.text: It's freezing outside. / You're gonna catch cold and then everyone's going to get sick in the bunker. / What are you talking about, freezing? It's sunny as shit.
jokes.setup: Dee complaining about Frank opening the car window
jokes.punchline: Frank pointing out it's actually sunny and nice outside, undermining the whole storm panic
jokes.explanation: Good reality check moment. The contrast between their panic and actual pleasant weather highlights their media-driven hysteria.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: It's freezing outside. / You're gonna catch cold and then everyone's going to get sick in the bunker.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What are you talking about, freezing? It's sunny as shit.

jokes.setup: Cricket saying he doesn't want to die
jokes.punchline: Frank threatening to cut Cricket in half while supposedly trying to save his life
jokes.explanation: Frank's casual violence threat while ostensibly helping is perfectly contradictory. Good character consistency with his sociopathic tendencies.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Don't make me do it, Cricket, I will slice you in half.

jokes.text: For the good of the race? / So come to the bar and, uh, oh, and I have a contr-- an agreement, and I need you to sign... / Storm's not going to hit Philly. It's just going to drizzle.
jokes.setup: Dennis awkwardly hitting on Jackie Denardo with his breeding philosophy
jokes.punchline: News crew casually revealing the storm is fake just as Dennis pulls out his creepy contract
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - Dennis's creepy pickup attempt gets interrupted by the reveal that the whole storm is fake. Great structural comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: For the good of the race? / So come to the bar and, uh, oh, and I have a contr-- an agreement, and I need you to sign...

dialogue.speaker: News Crew

jokes.text: How am I supposed to turn that into news? / I don't know, find some other way to make these people go nuts. We need a story.
jokes.setup: Learning the storm won't hit Philadelphia
jokes.punchline: News crew casually discussing how to manufacture fake panic for ratings
jokes.explanation: Sharp media criticism. The casual discussion of manufacturing panic for ratings exposes the cynical nature of news media.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie
dialogue.line: How am I supposed to turn that into news?

dialogue.speaker: Producer
dialogue.line: I don't know, find some other way to make these people go nuts. We need a story.

jokes.text: Maybe we can turn this into global warming. That always gets people whipped up, huh?
jokes.setup: News crew needing a story to replace the fake storm
jokes.punchline: Casually suggesting global warming as a reliable panic-inducer
jokes.explanation: Good media satire about how news outlets cycle through panic topics. The casual cynicism is well-observed.

dialogue.line: Maybe we can turn this into global warming. That always gets people whipped up, huh?

jokes.text: You're gonna treat me like a dog, huh? You're gonna treat Cricket like a dog?
jokes.setup: Dennis being rejected and called a creep
jokes.punchline: Cricket somehow relating this to his own mistreatment, making it about himself

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: You're gonna treat me like a dog, huh? You're gonna treat Cricket like a dog?

jokes.text: We can only accept cash at this time. / Oh, shit. / What? / Okay, well, you got cash? / I only have cards. / Charlie, where's the cash I gave you? / Mac took off with all our cash! He just took off!

jokes.punchline: Revealing Mac has abandoned them and stolen their money
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of their problems. Mac's betrayal is perfectly in character for someone who talks about loyalty constantly.

jokes.text: Loot, loot, time to loot, loot! Loot, loot! Take everything! Kill the machines! It's their fault! Kill the machines...!

jokes.punchline: Dennis leading a chant against machines while starting a riot

dialogue.line: Loot, loot, time to loot, loot! Loot, loot! Take everything! Kill the machines! It's their fault! Kill the machines...!

jokes.punchline: Charlie apparently kidnapping the weather woman during the riot

dialogue.line: Dennis, I got her, Dennis, I got her!

jokes.punchline: Frank still focused on the 3-D TV and breasts while everything falls apart
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending callback. Frank remaining focused on breasts while society collapses around him is peak character consistency.
